---
title: "Hands Dirty First"
subtitle: "Practical Notes from Before, During, and After Building Agentic Workflows"
author: "Peng Zhang · CIMS Global"
date: 2026-06-16
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
format:
  revealjs:
    output-file: index.html
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    progress: true
    history: true
    transition: slide
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    footer: "GenAI in Pharma 2026 — Virtual"
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <style>
            .title-logo {
              position: absolute;
              top: 24px;
              height: 80px;
              width: auto;
              z-index: 20;
            }
            .title-logo-left  { left: 24px; }
            .title-logo-right { right: 24px; }
          </style>
    include-before-body:
      - text: |
          <img src="image/logo.png" class="title-logo title-logo-left" alt="CIMS Global logo" />
          <img src="image/logo-rinpharma.png" class="title-logo title-logo-right" alt="R/Pharma logo" />
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script>
            function toggleTitleLogos(indexh) {
              const display = indexh === 0 ? "" : "none";
              document.querySelectorAll("img.title-logo").forEach(img => {
                img.style.display = display;
              });
            }
            Reveal.addEventListener("ready", e => toggleTitleLogos(e.indexh));
            Reveal.addEventListener("slidechanged", e => toggleTitleLogos(e.indexh));
          </script>
---

## {.bigtext}

This talk is presented in two different time zones and available [here](https://pzhang724.github.io/GenAI-in-Pharma-2026):

- **US session**
  - Peng Zhang,  Associate Director, Innovative Data Sciences; [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/peng-zhang-4522a0127), [Github](https://github.com/pzhang724/)
  - Internal development of R packages, R Shiny apps, agentic workflow, 
  - R for monitoring and submissions
  - SME for environment & platform development. 
- **APAC session** 
  - Mia Chen, Clinical Data Scientist; [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/in/chchen11/)
  - Clinical data engineering, R Shiny application development
  - AI-driven workflow automation for clinical research.

# Before — early exploration

## Where I'm coming from

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
From a LinkedIn post I wrote (Feb, 2026) — **six months of getting my hands dirty** before any of the workflow work:

- **Daily** practice with different AI assistants
- Follow **AI creators**; learn the *curated* news
- Then pick an **IDE**, spend **$20** on a pro agent, and work **inside the IDE and Terminal**
- Try different models and get familiar 

[Read the post on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/peng-zhang-4522a0127_finally-i-have-configured-claude-code-on-activity-7433203325115719680-gj3U){style="text-decoration: underline;"}
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](image/image.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

## Mindset Change

::: {.incremental}
- Previously, I used the agent to ask the question, refine the code for simple tasks 
- Early this year, watch the short courses from [Eric Zhang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/eric-pingye-zhang-b9382582/){style="text-decoration: underline;"} for **PharmaDS 2026**.
- [PharmaDS 2026 short courses](https://phds.nestat.org/short_courses.html){.smaller} 
- By talking to the agent, giving detailed information, it can search, analyze and generate review for previous public studies.
- Moreover, this can happen in a **personal workplace**.
:::

. . .


## What I saw

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](image/image-1.png){ height="300px" fig-align="center"}

*Provide information in the Claude Code*
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](image/image-2.png){height="500px" fig-align="center"}

*Clean and formatted output generated*
:::
:::

# During — observations after diving in

## Suddenly affordable

::: {.incremental}
- Anyone can run **Claude Code** or **Codex** right in the **terminal** on their PC, and with just a **$20** subscription.
  - **No need to build orchestration from scratch** — use it directly.
  - **Numerous resources** to connect (e.g., **ClinicalTrials.gov**).
  - **Hands-on**: build a workflow and try something yourself.
- Building an agentic workflow is so accessible
  - The **"Skill"** concept in Claude.
  - Instruct it with a **markdown file** and references.
  - The **context window** is large enough to absorb all the knowledge you provide.
:::

## Example 1 - A demo for [paperclip](https://gxl.ai/blog/paperclip)

A tool for scientific literature reivew, personally built them in **R Shiny** by different roles. [link](https://pzhang-link-claude-code-paperclip.share.connect.posit.cloud/)

![](image/image-4.png){fig-alt="Clinical AI Agent Shiny app showing a GLP-1 receptor agonist competitive landscape" width="66%" fig-align="center"}


## Example 2 — generating a mock shell

![](image/Animation.gif){fig-alt="Animation of Claude Code generating a mock shell" width="72%" fig-align="center"}

## Quick Personal Reflection

::: {.incremental}
- Sometimes the agent is better than me (Carefulness, Efficiency, Understanding in some aspects, etc.)
- More and more documents get written **for the agent** (e.g., **CLAUDE.md**)
- Fortunately, in our industry **humans stay accountable** for every asset.
- So I keep asking: what is the **minimum a human must still do**?
- And **what should stay human-readable**?
:::

## An interesting Experience

::: {.incremental}
- "Keep a human in the loop" **sounds like a safety guarantee.**
- A real experience: working on **Pilot 7** Synthetic Data Generation ([R Consortium synthetic-data submission](https://github.com/RConsortium/submissions-pilot7-synthetic-data)).
- Group Lead [Yilong Zhang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/yilongzhang/) asked: *can you generate SDTM and ADaM data from a source dataset in `.xml`?*
- **Why not?**
:::

## How I did it {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
- I created a docker for RStudio
- I install Claude Code in the RStudio (Yes you can use that through terminal from RStudio)
- I use Claude Code to:
  - Read the more than 90K line of data
  - Generate an html visualization
  - Separate the data by domain to have a sense on the data.
- Then ask agent to
  - Use reference from SDTM IG with index (more efficiently, saving window)
  - Write the SDTM spec into .yaml format (human and machine readable) for different domain
  - Write the R code based on .yaml file
  - Generate the SDTM dataset and checklogs, resolve all the coding errors
  - Do the same for ADaM Part. 
- [Pull Request](https://github.com/RConsortium/submissions-pilot7-synthetic-data/pull/5)
- Including Environment setup, this costs me around 5 hours totally for the initial draft.
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
![](image/image-3.png){fig-align="center"}


:::
:::

## But — am I really in the loop?

::: {.incremental}
- One uncomfortable truth: after generating the data, **I know almost nothing about it.**
- I can't be sure the **code** is right or the **data** is right.
- I only know: the **spec is written**, **code generated**, **no errors**, **data produced** 
— and **I'm a human in the loop.**
- Is *that* what "**human-in-the-loop**" should mean? **What are we missing?**
- The missing piece: an **immediate human review / approve / authorize** step to ensure correctness.
- **Still ongoing.** from the working group
:::

# After - Different Aspects to consider 

## Comparison

::: {.incremental}
- We might see lots of agentic workflow claim they are generating code/results in **a short period of time** (e.g. several hours)
- However, original programmatic solutions can also address this efficiently (e.g., TFL)
- So ask: **what are we comparing against by using AI?**
- It's a good way to use AI to **accelerate an existing, validated process** (maintained and validated in several years)
:::

## Evaluation

::: {.incremental}
- [Submission Working Group Pilot 7](https://github.com/RConsortium/pharma-skills)
    - Publicly Available agentic workflow in Pharma. 
    - Still ongoing 
- Recommendations 
  - Build your **benchmark** with test cases — *easy, medium, difficult*.
  - Define your **rubrics** — Compliance, Correctness, Completeness.
  - Run the **evaluation** on your agentic workflow.
  - **Collect the evidence** and results.
  - [Good References](https://agentskills.io/home)
:::

## Efficiency — data models matter

::: {.incremental}
- An inspiring thread of conversations with [Doug Kelkhoff](https://www.linkedin.com/in/doug-kelkhoff/).
- In the era of agents and LLMs, **data models may matter even more** — structured data that connects to unstructured context.
- This standard sits between your input and output.
- Benefit: **reliability and efficiency**.
:::



## Governance is the hard part

In a regulated environment, capability is the easy half. The other half:

::: {.incremental}
- **Data privacy** — HIPAA, GDPR
- **Confidential information & trade secrets** — what can leave the building?
- **Where does the model run?** — trust in **Anthropic vs Azure vs Amazon Bedrock**
:::


# Conclusion

## What I'd leave you with

::: {.incremental}
-  Get your hands dirty first — Try using terminal and different CLI.
-  Build a workflow for yourself and see what are available and what can be improved.
-  Domain knowledge still matter the most for the correctness; Accountability stays with people
-  Use dummy data and public available information, and take advantage of them.
:::


. . .

Thank you!
